← the Atlas

Sleeping terminals — on a first-class Tile

feature · seedling ·proposed

A plan of record for Sleep/Wake, re-grounded after a structural review (lowy + hickey). The blocker isn't sleep — it's that a tile is complected with a terminal (tileIds equal terminalIds), so a non-live tile has no home and a first attempt bolted it on as a silo. Fix the wart first — a first-class Tile registry with a TileContent union (terminal-only today), then sleeping is just a tile STATE — focusable, draggable, resizable, persisted and restored like any tile — with a frozen screenshot for its dormant body. Four phased PRs.

Contents

  • The wart — a tile is a live terminal
  • A first-class Tile PR 1
  • Sleeping is a tile state PR 2 · PR 3
    • A frozen screenshot for the dormant body PR 3
    • Sleep / Wake mechanism (reused wholesale)
  • Persisted and restored — tiles, not just terminals
  • The four phases

You asked for Sleep: leave a Claude Code terminal blocked for days, sleep it (its PTY, xterm, WebGL context, and agent all released — gone, like closing it), and wake it later in place with the agent resumed. A first cut shipped the mechanism — but built the sleeping tile as a parallel silo (its own component, its own dock section, a list it was excluded from). It worked, but it was wrong, and a structural review (lowy volatility + hickey simplicity) said why: there is no first-class “tile” in kolu. A tile is a live terminal. So a tile with no live terminal has nowhere to exist, and the only move left was to bolt one on beside the real ones.

This note is the re-plan. It fixes the wart first, then sleep falls out almost for free — and a future planned terminal comes for free after that. Four phased PRs.

The wart — a tile is a live terminal

The canvas calls itself “domain-agnostic — tiles, content injected via render props.” It isn’t, quite. Four identities are fused into “terminal”, so a tile cannot exist without a live one:

  • tileIds === terminalIds — the canvas iterates the live terminal list.
  • tile.layout === TerminalMetadata.canvasLayout (surface.ts:245) — a tile’s position is a field on the terminal.
  • activeTile === activeId, a terminal id — selection is keyed to terminals.
  • dock rows rank terminalIds — a tile that isn’t a live terminal is invisible.

The silo, named — both lenses independently flagged the same fingerprints

The first attempt proved the wart by reproducing it: a second <For> loop, a second layout store inside the sleeping record, and a split-brained reconciler (layoutOf/persistLayout branching on sleeping.getLayout(id) !== undefined). That branch is the smell — drag/resize/active should never know a tile’s kind. Both lenses also caught real latent bugs born of the parallel registry: pendingLayouts.dropEvicted never evicts a sleeping tile’s pending layout (it isn’t in the live set), and terminalCount + sleeping.records().length leaks the two-registry split into the canvas-mode decision. Fragmentation, not decomposition.

A first-class Tile PR 1

The fix is not an electricity (no @kolu/* package — a tile is app-domain state, hiding a bounded algorithm, not a transport-class volatility; the same verdict electricity.mdx already records for @kolu/geometry). It’s an in-app decomplect: separate tile presence from terminal liveness.

A Tile registry (useTileStore, fed by the existing terminal store) becomes the one source of truth for what the canvas, dock, and selection read:

The Tile ownsThe Terminal owns
identity (TileId), layout, active/selection, MRUPTY · xterm · agent · attach stream
a TileContent discriminated unionrepo/branch identity, agent state, the live body

TileContent is { kind: "terminal", terminalId } — and that is the only variant in PR 1. The canvas renders one <For> over tileStore.tiles() dispatching on kind; activeId becomes a TileId; focusedId narrows TileId → TerminalId only when kind === "terminal"; mode() keys off tiles.length. The reconciler disappears. Zero behavior change — Terminal is the only tile there is.

Why this is the load-bearing PR

This is the whole point of the re-plan: once a tile is a real thing with its own identity, layout, selection, and a content union, “sleeping” is a new union variant — it inherits drag, resize, focus, active, dock ordering, and persistence for free, because those operate on the content-agnostic registry. The silo becomes unrepresentable. A future planned terminal is then a third variant, not a third silo.

Sleeping is a tile state PR 2 · PR 3

With the registry in place, a sleeping terminal is a tile whose content is { kind: "sleeping", record } and whose liveness is false. It behaves no differently from a live terminal: same dock row (selectable — clicking focuses it, it can be the active tile, it never auto-wakes), same draggable and resizable canvas tile, same MRU. The only differences are a dormant body and one added verb — Wake (re-spawn PTY + resume the agent, flipping the content back to terminal).

A frozen screenshot for the dormant body PR 3

The dormant body shouldn’t be a bare card — it should be the terminal as you left it. Just before sleeping, capture a screenshot of the tile, write it to the state dir, and serve it back so the sleeping tile shows its frozen last frame (dimmed, with ☾ and Wake). It reuses the existing screenshotTerminal path; the new surface is a small served image route from KOLU_STATE_DIR (the serve-dir / iframe-preview route is the precedent), and the screenshot rides the sleeping record so it survives a restart.

Sleep / Wake mechanism (reused wholesale)

The freeze/resume machinery itself is settled and survives from the first cut — it’s session restore scoped to one tile:

Persisted and restored — tiles, not just terminals

Tiles must survive a reload and a restart, live or sleeping — that’s the whole point of sleeping for days. The model makes this explicit:

  • Live tiles persist + restore exactly as today: the session cell holds each terminal’s layout/theme/intent/agent, and session restore respawns them.
  • Sleeping tiles persist in their own on-disk sleepingTerminals store (disjoint from session, so the live-session autosave can’t clobber them) and rehydrate on boot as sleeping — never auto-woken. The frozen screenshot is part of that record.
  • On boot the Tile registry merges both sources into one tile list, so the canvas/dock see a single, ordered set of tiles — some live, some asleep — restored to their saved positions.

Layout's home — decided with the lenses

hickey argued canvasLayout should move off TerminalMetadata onto the tile record; lowy argued defer that schema migration (the client registry split needs no schema change — SleepingTerminal.id is already a stable tile id). We take lowy’s sequencing: PR 1 introduces the client-side Tile registry with no schema change (live-tile layout still reads from terminal metadata, sleeping-tile layout from the record — the registry hides which); a server-side unified tile store is a later, optional migration, only if a second consumer of “persisted tile position independent of liveness” appears. Don’t move the schema on spec.

The four phases

Each phase is one reviewable PR; each leaves master shippable.

PhasePRWhat landsWhy separable
1 — Tile #1435 useTileStore + TileContent union (terminal-only), one canvas <For> dispatching on content.kind, TileId selection, mode() on tiles.length, layout read/write through the registryPure decomplect, zero behavior change — reviewable on its own, de-risks everything after
2 — Sleeping statefeature{kind:"sleeping"} variant + sleep/wake RPCs + the persisted store + restore-as-sleeping; dock row + canvas tile via the shared pathsThe user-facing core; no silo, no new dock section
3 — Frozen screenshotfeaturecapture-on-sleep, served image route from the state dir, frozen bodyCohesive, isolated surface (a route + a capture)
4 — Louder alerts & parityfeaturethe awaiting/alert signals get louder everywhere (an escape hatch earns it), switcher parity, the 48h idle-nudgePolish; keeps PR 2 tight

Why the order is the point

Sleep was never the hard part — the machinery shipped in a day. The hard part was that there was nowhere clean to put a dormant tile. Phase 1 builds that place. Everything after is a variant, a body, or a signal — small, because the structure finally fits.

The open decisions, now narrow: whether the served screenshot route reuses iframePreviewRoute’s static-serve path or gets its own; whether a focused sleeping tile shows a hint that typing will offer to wake it; and the eventual server-side tile-store migration (deferred above). None block Phase 1.
